1:
"Then came the daughters of Zelophehad, the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph: and these are the names of his daughters; Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah." The very brief verse notes the daughters of Zelophehad coming with their claim. The grammatical voices note the petitioners. Henry and Gill note that "the daughters of Zelophehad... came... Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah" — the five daughters of Zelophehad, of the tribe of Manasseh, came forward with a claim concerning their father's inheritance; the the daughters of Zelophehad (the petitioners, a man who left no sons); the of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph (their tribe and descent); the these are the names of his daughters; Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah (the five named); the figure (the daughters coming forward in faith and order to seek their inheritance; the truth that even the women of Israel had a claim to the land of promise, and sought it; the figure of those who lay hold of the inheritance, the daughters honored for their faith in the promise; the names recorded for a memorial, their cause heard by God, vv.7).

On "then came the daughters of Zelophehad, the son of Hepher... of the families of Manasseh the son of Joseph," the witnesses note the petitioners. Gill notes the daughters of Zelophehad of Manasseh coming with their claim (the petitioners) — the petitioners (the daughters).

On "and these are the names of his daughters; Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the names: "Mahlah, Noah, and Hoglah, and Milcah, and Tirzah" — the five daughters named (recorded for a memorial); the figure (those who lay hold of the inheritance); the providence (the daughters coming, the claim stated to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the daughters of Zelophehad coming with their claim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the petitioners — "then came the daughters of Zelophehad... The petitioners display those who lay hold of the inheritance. The verse displays the daughters: petitioning. The verse displays the names: five. The verse displays the inheritance: sought. 2:
"And they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest, and before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, saying," The very brief verse notes the daughters standing before Moses and the assembly. The grammatical voices note the appearance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest, and before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle... saying" — the daughters presented their case openly before Moses, the priest, the princes, and the congregation; the they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest (before the leaders); the and before the princes and all the congregation (before the whole assembly); the by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation (the place of judgment, before God); the figure (the daughters bringing their cause openly and in order before the appointed authorities and before God; the truth that they sought their right in the lawful way, not by force but by petition; the figure of the orderly seeking of justice, the cause brought before God's appointed; the case laid before the LORD at the tabernacle door, vv.5).

On "they stood before Moses, and before Eleazar the priest," the witnesses note the appearance. Gill notes the daughters standing before the leaders (the lawful appeal) — the appearance (before Moses).

On "and before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, saying," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the assembly: "before the princes and all the congregation, by the door of the tabernacle" — the cause before the whole assembly and God (the place of judgment); the figure (the orderly seeking of justice); the providence (the appearance, the claim stated to follow, vv.3-4). The verse displays the daughters standing before Moses and the assembly.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 3:
"Our father died in the wilderness, and he was not in the company of them that gathered themselves together against the LORD in the company of Korah; but died in his own sin, and had no sons." The very brief verse notes the daughters' plea, their father not a rebel but dying without sons. The grammatical voices note the plea. Calvin and Gill note that "our father died in the wilderness, and he was not in the company of them that gathered themselves together against the LORD in the company of Korah; but died in his own sin, and had no sons" — the daughters pleaded that their father died not in rebellion with Korah, but in the common sin of his generation, leaving no sons; the our father died in the wilderness (the father's death among the fallen generation); the he was not in the company... of Korah (not a rebel, not cut off for rebellion); the but died in his own sin (in the common sin of unbelief, not in special rebellion); the and had no sons (the ground of the claim, no male heir); the figure (the careful plea that the father's name not be lost for a sin that did not forfeit the inheritance; the truth that the daughters distinguished their father from the rebels, that his line not be cut off; the figure of the concern for the inheritance and the name, the just distinction between the common sin and the rebellion that forfeits; the daughters' care that their father's portion be preserved, vv.4).

On "our father died in the wilderness, and he was not in the company of them that gathered themselves together against the LORD in the company of Korah," the witnesses note the distinction. Gill notes the father not a rebel with Korah (the distinction) — the plea (not in rebellion).

On "but died in his own sin, and had no sons," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ground: "died in his own sin, and had no sons" — the common sin, the lack of male heir (the ground of the claim); the figure (the inheritance not forfeited, the name preserved); the providence (the plea, the request to follow, vv.4). The verse displays the daughters' plea, their father not a rebel but without sons.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The plea displays the careful distinction that preserves the name. The verse displays the father: distinguished. The verse displays the inheritance: not forfeited. The verse displays the claim: just. The daughters' plea, their father not a rebel but without sons, displays the careful distinction: he was not in the company... of Korah; but died in his own sin, and had no sons — the daughters distinguishing their father from the rebels, that his line not be cut off, the inheritance not forfeited by the common sin. The verse displays their request that their father's name not be lost (v.4).
4:
"Why should the name of our father be done away from among his family, because he hath no son? Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father." The very brief verse notes the daughters' request for a possession to preserve their father's name. The grammatical voices note the request. Wesley and Gill note that "why should the name of our father be done away from among his family, because he hath no son? Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father" — the daughters asked that they be given an inheritance, that their father's name not perish for want of a son; the why should the name of our father be done away... because he hath no son? (the plea against the loss of the name); the Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father (the request for the inheritance); the figure (the request for the inheritance, that the name and portion be preserved; the truth that the concern for the name in Israel was the concern for the inheritance in the land of promise; the figure of those who seek their portion in the inheritance, the daughters' faith and care; the just petition that God would honor, that the name be not lost, vv.7). The verse holds together the request for the inheritance.

On "why should the name of our father be done away from among his family, because he hath no son?" the witnesses note the plea. Gill notes the plea against the loss of the father's name (the concern for the name) — the plea (the name preserved).

On "Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the request: "Give unto us therefore a possession among the brethren of our father" — the request for the inheritance (the portion sought); the figure (those who seek their portion in the inheritance); the providence (the request, Moses bringing the cause to God to follow, vv.5). The verse displays the daughters' request for a possession.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the request — "why should the name of our father be done away... The verse displays the request. The verse marks the name: to be preserved. The verse displays the possession: sought. The verse displays the inheritance: requested. The verse displays the faith: of the daughters. 5:
"And Moses brought their cause before the LORD." The very brief verse notes Moses bringing their cause before the LORD. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Calvin and Gill note that "Moses brought their cause before the LORD" — Moses, not deciding of himself, laid the daughters' case before God for his judgment; the Moses brought their cause before the LORD (the cause referred to God); the figure (the humility and faithfulness of Moses, not presuming to judge a new case but seeking God's word; the truth that the leader of God's people sought direction from God in what was not yet revealed; the figure of the cause committed to God, the matter referred to his judgment; the model of seeking God's will rather than one's own, "if any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God," James 1:5; the just cause brought before the righteous Judge). The verse holds together Moses bringing the cause to God.

On "and Moses brought their cause," the witnesses note the referral. Gill notes Moses referring the cause to God (not deciding himself) — the referral (the cause brought).

On "before the LORD," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note God's judgment: "before the LORD" — the cause referred to God's judgment (James 1:5); the figure (the cause committed to God); the providence (the appeal, God's answer to follow, vv.6-7). The verse displays Moses bringing their cause before the LORD.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 6:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD answering Moses concerning the daughters' claim. The grammatical voices note the answer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying" — God answered Moses, now to give his judgment on the daughters' cause (vv.7); the speaking (God's answer, the judgment given); the occasion (the cause referred to God, now his decision, that the daughters' claim was just); the figure (God answering the cause brought to him, giving his righteous judgment; the truth that God hears and answers the cause committed to him, the just claim granted; the figure of the answer of God to those who seek his will, the matter decided by his word; the LORD giving the law of inheritance through this case, vv.7-11). That God answers shows he hears the cause referred to him and gives righteous judgment.

On "the LORD spake unto Moses," the witnesses note the word. Gill notes the LORD answering Moses (the judgment) — the answer (God's word).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the judgment: the LORD spake unto Moses — the answer to the cause; the providence (God giving his righteous judgment). The verse displays the LORD answering Moses concerning the claim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," the judgment (vv.7, Keil, Gill); "the LORD spake unto Moses," the word (Gill); the judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the claim: to be granted. The answer displays God hearing the cause referred to him. The verse displays the LORD: answering. The verse displays the judgment: righteous. The verse displays the claim: just. The LORD answering Moses concerning the daughters' claim displays God's righteous judgment: the LORD spake unto Moses (vv.7) — God answering the cause referred to him, giving his righteous judgment that the daughters' claim was just. The verse displays the granting of the daughters' inheritance (v.7).
7:
"The daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren; and thou shalt cause the inheritance of their father to pass unto them." The witnesses note God's judgment that the daughters spoke right and should inherit — the crux of the just inheritance and the equity of God's law. On "the daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance," interpreters expound the judgment. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the just grant — "the daughters of Zelophehad speak right: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren": God declared the daughters' claim just and commanded that their father's inheritance pass to them; the witnesses note the significance — the equity of God's judgment, owning the daughters' right (God himself approving their cause, "they speak right," and securing their portion); the lesson — the justice and tenderness of God's law, providing for the daughters that the name and inheritance be not lost (the law of inheritance established through their case, that where there is no son, the daughters inherit, vv.8); the truth that God's law cares for the weak and the unprovided, securing the right of the fatherless daughters; the figure — the equity of God who judges righteously and gives to each their due, "shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?" Genesis 18:25; the figure of the inheritance given to the faithful, even the daughters, a token of the wideness of God's grace that makes heirs of those who seemed to have no portion, "if children, then heirs," Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29; The figure of the just inheritance secured by the equity of God's law.

On "the daughters of Zelophehad speak right," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the approval. Ellicott and Clarke note God owning the daughters' right, "they speak right" (the equity, the cause just, Genesis 18:25) — the approval (the cause owned).

On "thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren; and thou shalt cause the inheritance of their father to pass unto them," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the grant. Calvin and Gill note the daughters granted their father's inheritance, the portion secured (the just provision, the inheritance passing to them, Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29) — the figure (the inheritance secured to the faithful); the providence (the grant, the law of inheritance to follow, vv.8-11). The verse displays God's judgment that the daughters spoke right and should inherit.

The witnesses agree the substance: God declared the daughters' claim just and granted them their father's inheritance (Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the just inheritance and the equity of God's law: the daughters' right owned — God himself approving their cause ("they speak right") and securing their portion, the justice and tenderness of his law providing for the unprovided, that the name and inheritance be not lost, a figure of the equity of God who judges righteously (Genesis 18:25) and of the inheritance given to the faithful, making heirs of those who seemed to have no portion (Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29). The verse displays the just-inheritance crux. God's judgment that the daughters spoke right and should inherit displays the crux: thou shalt surely give them a possession of an inheritance among their father's brethren (Genesis 18:25; Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28-29) — God owning the daughters' just claim and securing their portion, the equity and tenderness of his law that provides for all his people, a figure of the inheritance given to the faithful. The verse displays the law of inheritance established (vv.8-11).
8:
"And thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a man die, and have no son, then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter." The very brief verse notes the law that the daughter inherits where there is no son. The grammatical voices note the law. Ellicott and Gill note that "if a man die, and have no son, then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter" — from the daughters' case God gave a general law: where there is no son, the daughter inherits; the if a man die, and have no son (the case of no male heir); the then ye shall cause his inheritance to pass unto his daughter (the daughter the heir); the figure (the general law of inheritance established from the particular case; the truth that God's law provided for the daughters, that the inheritance not be lost; the figure of the order of inheritance, the lasting statute drawn from the just cause; the equity of the law that secures the portion to the next of kin, beginning with the daughter; the provision for all in the inheritance of God's people, vv.9-11).

On "thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel... If a man die, and have no son," the witnesses note the case. Gill notes the case of no son (the law's occasion) — the law (no male heir).

The verse displays the law that the daughter inherits where there is no son.

The verse displays the further order of inheritance (vv.9-11).
9:

11:

12:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Get thee up into this mount Abarim, and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel." The very brief verse notes Moses bid to view the land from Mount Abarim. The grammatical voices note the command. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Get thee up into this mount Abarim, and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel" — God bid Moses ascend Abarim to see, but not enter, the promised land; the Get thee up into this mount Abarim (the ascent to the height); the and see the land which I have given unto the children of Israel (the land seen but not entered); the figure (Moses given to see the land from afar, the inheritance he might behold but not possess; the truth that Moses, for the sin at Meribah, would see the land but not enter it, vv.14; the figure of the law (in Moses) that brings to the border but cannot bring in, the land entered only by Joshua (Jesus); the tender mercy that let Moses see the land, "the LORD shewed him all the land," Deuteronomy 34:1; the inheritance given to Israel, beheld by the dying servant).

On "the LORD said unto Moses, Get thee up into this mount Abarim," the witnesses note the ascent. Gill notes Moses bid to ascend Abarim (the command to view) — the ascent (to the mount).

13:

The witnesses note Moses told he would be gathered to his people, as Aaron — the crux of the death of the servant who saw but did not enter. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses" — Moses laid his hands on Joshua and solemnly charged him, as God had commanded; the he laid his hands upon him (the laying on of hands, the ordination); the and gave him a charge (the solemn commission); the as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses (the obedience to God's command); the figure (the ordination of Joshua by the laying on of hands, the imparting of the Spirit and the charge; the truth that Joshua was full of the spirit of wisdom, "for Moses had laid his hands upon him," Deuteronomy 34:9; the figure of the ordination to office, the laying on of hands and the charge, the pattern of the solemn setting apart of God's servants, 1 Timothy 4:14; the orderly transfer of leadership completed, Joshua established as Moses's successor; the figure of the imparting of the Spirit and authority for the work of God). The verse closes the chapter, the succession secured.

On "he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge," the witnesses note the ordination. Gill notes Moses laying hands on Joshua and charging him (the ordination, Deuteronomy 34:9) — the ordination (the hands laid on).

On "as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses," the grammatical-historical Clarke and grammatical-historical note the obedience: "as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses" — Moses obeying God's command (the ordination as commanded, 1 Timothy 4:14); the figure (the solemn setting apart of God's servant); the providence (the succession secured, before the offerings of ch.28). The verse displays Moses laying his hands on Joshua and giving him the charge.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ordination — "he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses," the ordination (Keil, Gill); "he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge," the ordination (Deuteronomy 34:9, Gill); "as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses," the obedience (1 Timothy 4:14, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ordination. The verse marks Moses: laying hands. The verse displays Joshua: charged. The verse displays the Spirit: imparted. The verse displays the succession: secured. The ordination displays the solemn setting apart of God's servant. The verse displays the hands: laid on. The verse displays the charge: given. The verse displays the successor: established. Moses laying his hands on Joshua and giving him the charge displays the ordination of God's servant: he laid his hands upon him, and gave him a charge, as the LORD commanded (Deuteronomy 34:9; 1 Timothy 4:14) — Moses ordaining Joshua by the laying on of hands and the charge, the Spirit and authority imparted, the succession secured, Joshua established as the shepherd of God's people. The verse closes the chapter, before the law of the daily and festal offerings (ch.28).
